questions.question: What is the main purpose of the Temporal Docs MCP Server?

choices.text: It runs your Temporal Workflows in the cloud automatically
It gives AI assistants real-time access to Temporal documentation so their answers are based on current docs rather than old training data
It translates Temporal code from one programming language to another
It monitors your Temporal application and sends alerts when something goes wrong

questions.explanation: The Temporal Docs MCP Server acts as a bridge between AI tools and Temporal's documentation. Without it, an AI assistant can only answer questions based on whatever it learned during training, which may be outdated. By connecting to the MCP server, the AI can look up current Temporal documentation in real time, giving you more accurate and up-to-date answers about Temporal concepts and features.

questions.question: What is the difference between the 'Temporal Developer Skill' and the 'Temporal Cloud Skill'?

choices.text: The Developer Skill is for Python only, while the Cloud Skill works with all programming languages
The Developer Skill teaches AI agents how to write Temporal code, while the Cloud Skill helps AI agents troubleshoot connectivity and configuration issues with Temporal Cloud
The Developer Skill is free, while the Cloud Skill requires a paid subscription
There is no difference — they are two names for the same tool

questions.explanation: Temporal offers two distinct Skills for AI coding agents, each with a different focus. The Temporal Developer Skill gives an AI agent deep knowledge about how to build with Temporal — things like how Workflows work, retry policies, and error handling. The Temporal Cloud Skill, on the other hand, is specialized for helping diagnose problems when connecting to or configuring Temporal Cloud, such as authentication issues. Think of them as two different areas of expertise you can give your AI assistant.

questions.question: Which programming languages can you use to get started with Temporal, based on the available quickstarts?

choices.text: Only Go, Java, and Python
Go, Java, PHP, Python, Ruby, TypeScript, and .NET (C#)
Any programming language, because Temporal has no language restrictions
Only languages that support object-oriented programming

questions.explanation: Temporal provides official quickstart guides for seven programming languages: Go, Java, PHP, Python, Ruby, TypeScript, and .NET (C#). Each quickstart walks you through installing the appropriate SDK and running a simple 'Hello World' Workflow. An SDK (Software Development Kit) is a library that lets you write Temporal applications in your chosen language. While Temporal itself is language-agnostic at its core, you need an SDK for your specific language to interact with it.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Temporal Developer Skill when installed in an AI coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor?

choices.text: It connects the agent to a live Temporal Cloud instance to execute workflows on your behalf
It gives the agent expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model, including workflow determinism rules, activity patterns, retry policies, and common gotchas
It automatically generates Temporal workflow code from plain English descriptions and deploys it to your local Temporal server
It provides real-time access to the Temporal documentation website by acting as a proxy to the docs MCP server

questions.explanation: The Temporal Developer Skill is a domain-specific knowledge package that gives AI coding agents expert-level understanding of Temporal concepts — things like workflow determinism, activity patterns, retry policies, error handling, testing strategies, worker configuration, and versioning. It is distinct from the Temporal Docs MCP Server, which provides real-time documentation access. You would use this skill when you want your AI assistant to write correct Temporal code from the start, avoiding common pitfalls like non-deterministic workflow code.

questions.question: When adding the Temporal Docs MCP Server to Claude Code for use across ALL your projects, which command is correct?

choices.text: claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
claude mcp add --scope project --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
npx skills add https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai --scope global

questions.explanation: To make the Temporal Docs MCP Server available globally across all your Claude Code projects, you must include the '--scope user' flag. Omitting this flag (option A) stores the configuration only in the current project's .mcp.json file, making it project-specific rather than global. Option B uses '--scope project' which is not the documented flag, and option D confuses the skills CLI with the MCP server setup. Use '--scope user' when you want consistent Temporal docs access regardless of which project you're working in.

questions.question: What authentication method does the Temporal Docs MCP Server require, and why?

choices.text: It requires a Temporal Cloud API key to verify you are a paying customer before granting documentation access
It requires anonymous authentication using any Google account to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse, without collecting contact information
It requires creating a free account on temporal.io and generating a personal access token
No authentication is required; the server is fully public and open to anyone with the URL

questions.explanation: The Temporal Docs MCP Server uses anonymous authentication via a Google account solely to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse. Importantly, Temporal states they cannot see nor collect any contact information from this process. This is a common pattern for public APIs that need basic throttling without building a full user management system. You would encounter this authentication step after restarting Claude Code and running the '/mcp' command for the first time.

questions.question: A developer wants to install the Temporal Developer Skill using the npx CLI approach. Which command should they run?

choices.text: npx skills add temporal-developer@temporal-marketplace
npx temporal-skills install https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer
npx skills add https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer
npm install -g @temporalio/skill-temporal-developer

questions.explanation: The correct npx command is 'npx skills add https://github.com/temporalio/skill-temporal-developer'. This uses the 'skills' CLI via npx and passes the full GitHub URL of the skill repository. Option A uses the marketplace syntax which is specific to the Claude Code plugin installation method. Option B uses a non-existent 'temporal-skills' package name. Option D confuses this with a standard npm package installation. The npx approach is compatible with Claude Code, Codex, Cline, and other agents that support the Skills standard, and you must restart your coding agent after installing for the skill to take effect.

questions.question: Your team is using an AI coding agent to help write Temporal workflows. The agent was trained on data from 2023 and confidently generates workflow code, but you notice it keeps producing patterns that violate determinism rules introduced in newer SDK versions. What is the most targeted solution to give the agent accurate, current Temporal expertise?

choices.text: Manually paste relevant Temporal documentation into each prompt before asking the agent to write workflow code
Install the Temporal Developer Skill and connect the Temporal Docs MCP Server so the agent has both domain expertise and real-time documentation access
Switch to a newer AI model with a more recent training cutoff, since the core issue is stale training data
Have a senior Temporal engineer review all AI-generated code before it reaches production, relying on human expertise to catch errors

questions.explanation: The root problem has two distinct dimensions: (1) the agent lacks deep Temporal domain knowledge about determinism rules, retry policies, and common gotchas, and (2) its training data may not reflect current SDK behavior. The Temporal Developer Skill addresses the first problem by giving the agent expert-level knowledge of Temporal's programming model — including workflow determinism rules. The Temporal Docs MCP Server addresses the second problem by providing real-time access to current documentation rather than relying on training data. Using only one without the other leaves a gap. Switching AI models (C) doesn't guarantee current Temporal knowledge. Manual pasting (A) is unsustainable at scale. Human review (D) is a good safety net but doesn't fix the root cause — the agent will keep generating the same problematic patterns.

questions.question: A developer installs the Temporal Developer Skill for their AI coding agent and immediately asks it to generate a workflow. The agent produces incorrect output that ignores the skill's guidance. What is the most likely cause of this failure?

choices.text: The Temporal Developer Skill only works with Claude Code and not with other agents
Skills require a paid Temporal Cloud subscription to activate
The coding agent was not restarted after installing the skill, so the skill was not loaded into the agent's context
The skill must be re-installed for each new project directory before it takes effect

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly states 'Restart your coding agent after installing.' This is a common operational mistake — developers install plugins or skills and immediately try to use them without restarting the host application. Skills are loaded at agent startup, so any skill installed during a session won't be active until the agent process restarts and loads the new skill configuration. This same pattern applies to many plugin systems: the installation modifies configuration files on disk, but the running process must restart to read those files. Option A is false — the docs show Skills work with Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Cline, and any agent supporting the Skills protocol. Option B is false — there is no mention of a paid requirement. Option D is false — the installation is global or project-scoped based on how you install it, not per-directory.

questions.question: Your organization wants AI assistants to answer Temporal questions based on the latest documentation rather than potentially outdated training data. You configure the Temporal Docs MCP Server. A team member raises a concern: 'Won't Temporal be able to see our internal queries and sensitive workflow logic we ask about?' How should you respond based on how the MCP server is designed?

choices.text: Yes, Temporal logs all queries to improve the documentation — teams should avoid including proprietary workflow logic in questions
The server uses anonymous authentication via Google account only to enforce rate limits; Temporal states it cannot see nor collects any contact information
Queries are encrypted end-to-end and Temporal has no access to either the questions or the user identity
Enterprise customers can request a private MCP server deployment that logs nothing, but the default public server does log queries

questions.explanation: The documentation is precise about this: 'The server requires anonymous authentication using any Google account to enforce rate limits and prevent abuse. We cannot see nor do we collect any contact information from this.' The authentication serves a specific, limited purpose — rate limiting — not identification or data collection. This is an important distinction for teams evaluating whether to use the MCP server in enterprise contexts. The answer is neither the extreme of 'fully private with zero visibility' (C) nor 'actively logging your queries' (A or D). Understanding this nuance helps teams make informed decisions: the MCP server sees your queries insofar as it must serve documentation responses, but Temporal is not collecting your identity or stated intentions. Teams with highly sensitive requirements should still evaluate their own policies independently.

questions.question: A platform team wants to add the Temporal Docs MCP Server to Claude Code so it is available across ALL projects for every engineer on their machine, not just in a specific repository. Which command accomplishes this correctly?

choices.text: claude mcp add --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
claude mcp add --scope project --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
claude mcp add --scope user --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai
claude mcp add --global --transport http temporal-docs https://temporal.mcp.kapa.ai

questions.explanation: The documentation explicitly explains the scoping behavior: 'Add the Temporal docs MCP server globally so it's available in all your projects' uses the `--scope user` flag. Without `--scope user`, the command 'stores the configuration in the project's `.mcp.json` file' — making it project-specific. This scoping distinction matters in production: project-scoped MCP config (`.mcp.json`) is great for repositories where you want the config checked into version control so all contributors automatically get it. User-scoped config is better when you want it available everywhere on your machine without modifying every repository. Option A omits the scope flag entirely, which defaults to project scope per the docs. Option B explicitly sets project scope, which is the opposite of the goal. Option D uses `--global` which is not the documented flag — the correct flag is `--scope user`.

choices.text: The `continue-as-new` call passes an empty slice `[]Order{}` instead of the remaining unprocessed orders, meaning after a `continue-as-new` event, all order processing history is lost and orders will be silently skipped — but this is a business logic bug, not a determinism violation.

`processOrder` is called directly as a function rather than as an activity via `workflow.ExecuteActivity`. This means its execution is not recorded in the event history, so on replay the worker re-executes the function's side effects rather than replaying the recorded result — causing non-deterministic behavior if the function does any I/O, and potentially corrupting workflow state on worker restarts or upgrades.

The `ContinueAsNewSuggested` flag is checked inside the loop rather than after it, meaning the workflow could trigger `continue-as-new` mid-batch. This creates a determinism violation because the server expects the full batch to complete before the workflow terminates.

The slice `[]Order{}` passed to `NewContinueAsNewError` must be serializable via Temporal's data converter, and passing a Go slice directly without a wrapper struct will cause a serialization panic on the Temporal server side during history persistence.

questions.explanation:
The critical flaw is that `processOrder` is invoked as a plain Go function rather than being scheduled as a Temporal Activity via `workflow.ExecuteActivity`. This violates Temporal's core determinism contract.

**Why this matters architecturally:** Temporal's durability guarantee works by recording every significant event — activity scheduled, activity completed, timer fired, signal received — into an append-only event history. When a worker crashes and restarts, the Temporal SDK replays this history to reconstruct the exact workflow state without re-executing side effects. The SDK intercepts calls like `workflow.ExecuteActivity` and, during replay, returns the previously recorded result instead of actually executing the activity.

When you call a plain Go function from workflow code, the SDK has no knowledge of it. The function executes unconditionally on every replay — whether that means hitting a database, calling an external API, or generating a timestamp. If the function's output differs between the original execution and a replay (network failure, time-based logic, randomness, changed business logic), the workflow state diverges. Temporal detects some of these via non-determinism errors, but many silent corruptions slip through.

**The continue-as-new interaction makes this worse:** Since `continue-as-new` starts a fresh history, any state that was only tracked in plain function calls (not in the event history) is silently lost. The new workflow execution has no record of what `processOrder` did or returned.

**What the AI skill should have flagged:** The Temporal Developer Skill is specifically documented as covering 'workflow determinism rules, activity patterns' and 'common gotchas.' Calling I/O-performing functions directly in workflow code is the canonical non-determinism gotcha. The correct pattern is `workflow.ExecuteActivity(ctx, processOrder, order)` which records the scheduling and result in history.

**Answer A** describes a real business logic bug (data loss across continue-as-new boundaries) but is not a determinism violation. **Answer C** is incorrect — `ContinueAsNewSuggested` is checked after the loop body executes for each order, and triggering continue-as-new between items is valid as long as remaining work is passed forward. **Answer D** is false — Go slices are serializable via Temporal's default JSON data converter.

questions.question:
An engineering team is building a document processing pipeline using Temporal. They configure their AI coding agent with both the Temporal Developer Skill AND the Temporal Docs MCP Server. The agent produces the following Python worker setup:

```python
# worker.py
worker = Worker(
    client,
    task_queue='doc-processing',
    workflows=[DocumentPipelineWorkflow],
    activities=[extract_text, classify_document, store_result],
)

# workflow.py  
@workflow.defn
class DocumentPipelineWorkflow:
    @workflow.run
    async def run(self, doc_id: str) -> str:
        text = await workflow.execute_activity(
            extract_text,
            doc_id,
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=30),
        )
        category = await workflow.execute_activity(
            classify_document,
            text,
            start_to_close_timeout=timedelta(seconds=60),
        )
        result = await workflow.execute_activity(
            store_result,
            schedule_to_close_timeout=timedelta(minutes=5),
        )
        return result
```

The team asks the AI agent (with MCP Server access to current Temporal docs) to review this for production readiness. The agent flags one issue as a 'critical correctness bug' versus two issues as 'production reliability concerns.' Which of the following correctly identifies what the critical correctness bug is — the one that would cause silent data corruption or wrong results in production, as distinct from reliability issues like insufficient retry configuration?

choices.text: The `extract_text` activity receives `doc_id` as a positional argument rather than as a keyword argument. Temporal's Python SDK serializes activity arguments positionally, so passing `doc_id` this way will silently serialize it as `None` on the worker side if the activity signature uses keyword-only arguments.

`store_result` is called with `schedule_to_close_timeout` but without passing the required `category` and `text` arguments. The activity will execute but receive no input data, silently storing an empty or default result — this is a correctness bug because the workflow returns a result that does not reflect the processed document.

Using both `start_to_close_timeout` on `extract_text` and `schedule_to_close_timeout` on `store_result` in the same workflow is not supported by the Temporal Python SDK. When mixed timeout types are detected at registration time, the worker raises a `WorkflowTaskFailedError` that poisons the entire task queue.

The `classify_document` activity passes the full extracted `text` string as an activity argument. Since Temporal serializes this via the data converter and stores it in workflow history, extremely large documents will cause history size limit violations, which is a correctness bug because the workflow will be force-terminated by the server.

questions.explanation:
The critical correctness bug is that `store_result` is invoked without passing `category` or `text` as arguments. In Temporal's Python SDK, `workflow.execute_activity` passes arguments to the activity function. When no arguments are provided, the activity receives nothing (or Python defaults/None), meaning it cannot store the correct result. The workflow then returns whatever `store_result` produces from empty input — silently returning wrong data to the caller with no error raised.

**Why this is correctness, not reliability:** Reliability concerns (insufficient retry policies, missing heartbeating for long activities, no `schedule_to_start_timeout` to detect queue saturation) cause workflows to fail loudly or become stuck — problems that monitoring catches. A correctness bug produces a completed workflow with a wrong result. The workflow history shows SUCCESS, the caller receives a value, but the stored document category is wrong or empty. This class of bug is the most dangerous in production because it is invisible to alerting.

**The MCP Server advantage here:** The Temporal Docs MCP Server is documented as giving AI tools 'real-time access to our documentation, so responses draw from current docs rather than training data.' This matters because SDK argument-passing semantics and activity signature requirements are exactly the kind of detail that changes between SDK versions and that an AI trained on older data might misrepresent. An MCP-connected agent can retrieve the current Python SDK docs for `execute_activity` and confirm that arguments are passed as positional args after the activity reference.

**Answer A** is false — Temporal's Python SDK serializes arguments as a JSON array positionally, and passing `doc_id` as shown is the correct pattern; there is no silent None behavior from positional passing. **Answer C** is false — mixing `start_to_close_timeout` and `schedule_to_close_timeout` is fully supported and common; they apply to different activities independently. **Answer D** describes a real operational risk (history size limits are real, defaulting to 50MB per workflow) but it is a reliability/scalability concern, not a silent correctness bug — the server returns an explicit error when history limits are exceeded, making the failure visible.

**The production lesson:** Always audit activity call sites to verify arguments are passed correctly. Temporal's type system does not enforce this at compile time in Python, and the activity receives None or defaults silently. Code review and the workflow test suite (using `workflow.testing`) are the primary defenses.
